tweets.text: This is the first week where @Strategy's actions lend a hint that they understand investor concerns and are willing to address them. This is exactly what we've been advocating for - use $MSTR issuance to raise cash to bolster the balance sheet. There's still a ways to go that should keep pressure on $MSTR, but these actions will benefit not only the rest of the capital structure above the common, but also BTC itself.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Strategy has increased its USD Reserve by $300 million to $1.4 billion and plans to continue replenishing it to support the credit quality of its Digital Credit securities. We also acquired 520 BTC for $35 million, increasing our $BTC Reserve to ₿847,363. $MSTR $STRC.

tweets.text:
You could have said the same for Sol a year and a half in. Hype is still in its infancy. If you compared at the same points in time for each. Then it looks a bit different. 

You may find some existing tradfi companies use HL in the future in the backend pending CFTC for perps. Which makes it a lot more valuable. Or maybe that approval kills it. Idk. 

Not to mention revenue is growing and it’s not fully depending on perps. Yield from treasuries will add 500k per day soon to buybacks. 

It’d be like saying investing in binance was bad bc it’s just an exchange. 

In terms of FDV. The community bucket is unlikely to ever hit the market. What’s solanas FDV? It’s infinite. It’s growing at 1.8B a year even at these prices of new supply. There’s really not much incentive to hold sol itself outside of short term speculation 

If you look at the sol marketcap ath it basically 2x 2021 while price hit the 21 levels. 

Hype made a new all time high in price by far But marketcap was the same as the prev marketcap. There’s actual value accrual to the holders. 

Do I expect a 10x for either? No. Those days are gone. Do I think they should be compared? No. Sol and hype are two different things.

tweets.text:
Volume can be tricky as a single metric.

This is a number that venues publish themselves. As a result, it can be inflated or manipulated.

I think a much better metric is turnover, which essentially measures volume relative to OI and shows whether positions are being held or churned.

Here's how perp platforms rank by turnover:

→ @HyperliquidX: 0.38x
→ @variational_io: 0.61x
→ @Lighter_xyz: 0.97x
→ @edgeX_exchange: 1.18x
→ @grvt_io: 2.29x
→ @JupiterExchange: 2.71x

Lower ratio = sticky positions.
Higher ratio = more churn.

At the top of the list, Hyperliquid holds $9.4B in OI, more than the other five venues combined.

Jupiter has the highest turnover, but it’s a pool venue, so every trade settles against shared liquidity instead of other traders. High turnover is just how pool venues work, and the wash checks confirm the activity is real.

H/t @ax1vc for data.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Dune Dashboard for the Perp DEX Market, 2026

The majority of dashboards for perps just replicate the volumes the venue claims.

We built one that breaks down which of those figures the chain actually supports.

Six venues, head-to-head
@HyperliquidX, @Lighter_xyz,

→ @variational_io: 0.61x
→ @Lighter_xyz: 0.97x
→ @edgeX_exchange: 1.18x
→ @grvt_io: 2.29x
→ @JupiterExchange: 2.71x

Lower ratio = sticky positions.
Higher ratio = more churn.

At the top of the list, Hyperliquid holds $9.4B in OI, more than the other five venues combined.

Jupiter has the highest turnover, but it’s a pool venue, so every trade settles against shared liquidity instead of other traders. High turnover is just how pool venues work, and the wash checks confirm the activity is real.

H/t @ax1vc for data.

tweets.text:
RWA is one of the few crypto sectors that has had a good run this year.

The onchain RWA market is ~$32B now (~300% in a year). Almost all of that growth happened during one of the worst bear markets.

It starts to get interesting when you break down that market into categories, since the growth isn't spread evenly across each one:

→ Tokenized funds: ~80% of the market
→ Commodities: ~16% (mostly gold)
→ Tokenized stocks: under 4% (fastest growth)

I'm personally focused on the tokenized stocks sector. It's the smallest category, but the most useful for users, since you can buy and trade tokenized stocks like Tesla or Nvidia onchain 24/7.

And there's already a big demand for it. Projects like @xStocksFi and @OndoPerps already let anyone outside the US trade tokenized Tesla, Nvidia, or the S&P 500 onchain, 24/7, without a brokerage account.

Alongside neobanks, this is one of the few crypto sectors I’m still genuinely excited about.

Taiko chain state verification compromised

Bridges deployed on Taiko might be unreliable.

The ERC20 Vault was exploited for over $1M in losses, and Taiko has paused affected systems and asked CEXs to suspend TAIKO deposits.

When will this come to an end?
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
⚠️ Security Notice

1/2: We have confirmed a compromise of Taiko’s chain state verification mechanism. As a result, the security assumptions of all bridges deployed on Taiko can no longer be relied upon.

We are actively coordinating with the Security Council and ecosystem

thread_tweets.text: @KTmBoyle The critical change was when there started to be a path for startups to build very serious hardware -- not just consumer electronics, but things like supersonic planes and fusion reactors. Once you have that, defense tech is just a question of who you sell to.
thread_tweets.full_text: @KTmBoyle The critical change was when there started to be a path for startups to build very serious hardware -- not just consumer electronics, but things like supersonic planes and fusion reactors. Once you have that, defense tech is just a question of who you sell to.

tweets.text:
$msUSD was a good reminder that stablecoins are not all the same.

A high APY can look great on a dashboard…until the peg starts slipping and everyone tries to exit at the same time.

With stablecoins, the price is only one part of the story.

You also need to know the exit liquidity, backing quality, freeze risk, peg history and whether the yield is actually worth the risk underneath.

I started digging into this after the recent depeg drama and came across @PharosWatch.

It tracks 369 stablecoins across peg health, liquidity, freeze risk, safety scores, depeg warnings, and yield risk.

The part I liked most is the Yield Intelligence page. Instead of just showing raw APY, it maps stablecoin yields against safety and risk.

That’s useful because a 20% APY on a weak stablecoin isn't free yield.

Sometimes it’s just the market paying you to hold something fragile.

It also has a Safety Scores section that works like a stablecoin report card that helps identify hidden fragility underneath.

If you’re parking size into stables, farming yield, or rotating into newer RWA / treasury-backed coins, this is the kind of thing worth checking before the chart breaks.

Because stablecoins don’t usually feel risky. Until they become the whole trade.

tweets.text:
There have recently been claims that AI text analysis will make online anonymity untenable.

So let me cannibalize a piece of my own anonymity to do an experiment.

At some point this decade, I wrote a published document of medium importance to Ethereum - I estimate ~200 to 2000 documents in Ethereum are as or more important - not under my name.

Find it.

(I genuinely have no idea how easy or hard this is, will be very curious what comes out)

tweets.text:
𝗪𝗵𝗲𝗿𝗲 𝗜𝘀 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗔𝗹𝗽𝗵𝗮?

One of the most attractive hunting grounds for special situations is the $500 million to $3 billion market cap range.

Below $500 million, many companies still have unproven products, financing risks, or business models that may never work. Above a few billion dollars, tens of analysts, institutions, and algorithms are already competing to find every inefficiency.

Between $500 million and $3 billion sits a sweet spot. The companies are usually large enough to have a real product, real customers, and a proven business model, yet small enough that only a handful of specialists truly understand the story.

When sentiment turns negative, the market can dramatically misprice these businesses.

A few examples from the past 12 months:

Two of the stocks below doubled after my recommendation, one more than tripled, and one is currently up roughly 50%. The only one that has not doubled yet is $WGS, which I believe will eventually join the others.

$URGN traded around $11 before a negative FDA advisory committee vote sent the stock down to roughly $6.50.

The FDA later approved the product. In biotech, a successful approval often leads to a significant re-rating. What made this situation unusual was that the stock only moved to around $10 after approval.

That made little sense. Before the committee meeting and before the FDA approval, investors were already willing to value the company at roughly $11. The company had now received the approval that mattered most, yet the stock was trading below where it had traded before either event.

Immediately after the FDA approval, I wrote that the market reaction made very little sense(see tweet below). Here was a profitable company with an approved product and a strong competitive position, yet the market was valuing it below where it traded before receiving the approval. I published multiple articles explaining the disconnect and ultimately allocated roughly 10% of my own portfolio to the position.

The stock quickly moved toward $15 and eventually above $30.

$STAA collapsed after demand from China weakened and sentiment turned extremely negative. Yet the company still had a strong competitive position, a significant moat, and a product backed by decades of clinical data.

An acquisition proposal around $30.75 per share was rejected by some of the most knowledgeable shareholders in the sector, who believed the company was worth substantially more. Demand from China later recovered, sentiment reversed, and the stock more than doubled from the levels where I recommended it.

$ZIM traded around $15 when I recommended it. At the time, the company had roughly $22 per share in cash and a net asset value of around $60 per share. The highest price target among the six analysts covering the stock was $16, while the lowest was $6. Not a single analyst had a Buy rating.

Management was sandbagging expectations and doing little to highlight the company’s underlying value. Eventually, the market began to recognize the disconnect, and the stock more than doubled from my initial recommendation if you take into account dividends.

$WGS is my newest idea from this group. The stock fell roughly 50% after reimbursement changes despite owning one of the most valuable genomic datasets in the world. The market became fixated on a reimbursement issue while largely ignoring the long-term value of the business. The irony is that whole genome sequencing, which analyzes essentially the entire genome, was increasingly replacing whole exome sequencing, which examines only a small fraction of a patient’s DNA, yet genome testing was often reimbursed at a lower rate. In my view, the market focused on a short-term reimbursement distortion while overlooking the long-term value of the business and its data assets.  

The common theme is simple: these companies are large enough to matter but small enough to be misunderstood.

That is where some of the best alpha still exists.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
$URGN & Biotech Valuations

In biotech, broker reports often show 100–300% upside. That makes sense—pre–Phase 3, it’s all just a dream. Most fail. So those lofty targets really mean: “Here’s what it’s worth if they pass.”

Well, $URGN passed.

It moved from dream to reality—a

tweets.text:
If you put your goals in the hands of RPs who can execute those goals well, and if you make it clear to them that they are personally responsible for achieving those goals and doing the tasks, they should produce excellent results.

The same goes for yourself. If your designer/manager-you doesn't have a good reason to be confident that your worker-you is up to a given task, it would be crazy to let yourself do the task without seeking the supervision of believable parties. You know that there are a lot of incompetent people in the world trying to do things they're not good at, so the chances are good that you are one of them. That's just a reality and it's okay for you to accept it and deal with it in a way that produces good outcomes. #principleoftheday

tweets.text:
I am so proud to announce the launch of DA8, another $300M we are putting to work into disruptive early-stage founders!

41 years in, and I love the job more every day.

We relaunched @DraperVC 12 years ago. Since day one, we have continued to back founders who are willing to sound crazy. The ones who look at a broken industry, that hasn't been innovated on in 20+ years, and say, "I'm going to fix that, no matter what it takes."

Over the history of Draper, we have been the first VC in companies like @Tesla, @SpaceX, Hotmail, @Twitch, @Skype, @Baidu_Inc., Bitcoin, @Coinbase, @Robinhood, @Oklo, @Polymarket, @XanaduAI, @iceye_global, @Colossal, and the list goes on.

I remember the first time I met every one of those entrepreneurs, and the hundreds more we have funded over the years. At Draper, we look forward to funding more entrepreneurs who build unicorns and trinocorns (trillion dollar companies).

What I've learned after four decades is simple. The greatest founders are those who feel that they have to do what they are doing. It was pre-ordained.

Getting to help them and fund them early is the privilege of my work.

To our new Limited Partners: we appreciate you for working with us on this important mission. We know it is only the boldest LP that sees the investment world as we do.

To the founders coming to pitch us for DA8: we cannot wait to see the future you create.

And to anyone building something the world isn't ready for yet, keep going. We are looking for you.

Here's to DA8.

tweets.text:
I hope this is going to be the final lesson to stop aping any cash Strategy has in their hands at local highs right before panic selling for cash -35% lower

But I doubt that, because it's the second time they do it in one year after the USD reserve creation

That being said, we're in a catch-22 situation because a lot of people don't want to buy BTC before Saylor is out, and at the same time, it's quite unlikely to get him out because Strategy can just keep raising more cash and become extremely hard to attack
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Strategy has increased its USD Reserve by $300 million to $1.4 billion and plans to continue replenishing it to support the credit quality of its Digital Credit securities. We also acquired 520 BTC for $35 million, increasing our $BTC Reserve to ₿847,363. $MSTR $STRC

tweets.text:
@maruushae Sure, but volume is quantified way to measure mindshare around a token

Low volume potentially implies 2 things: 
1/ bullish: it definitely is far from saturating attention
2/ bearish: volume is too low because no one cares

tweets.text:
I'd imagine it would follow the same process as rough consensus today. i.e., core devs, community, and then validators who run nodes. 

Kinda like the game of hot potato, where everyone signals support, before its incorporated into the hard-fork. 

It will probably be messy, but I think that fight is better in hard-forks on eligibility of funding than it is on validators themselves.

thread_tweets.text:

Kinda like the game of hot potato, where everyone signals support, before its incorporated into the hard-fork. 

It will probably be messy, but I think that fight is better in hard-forks on eligibility of funding than it is on validators themselves.

thread_tweets.text:
Maybe on this -- I think it a proposal like this could solve the 'second foundation issue'.

i.e., if @dannyryan was interested in setting up a foundation, he could get the recipient address included in a hard-fork, then stakers could fund it, enabling a community-funded Foundation to emerge. 

Kinda like DAOs, where they can 'go over the heads of the founding team', but it should be a mammoth effort to do so.
